name: Dysprosium
name_origin: From Greek 'dysprositos' meaning 'hard to obtain', reflecting the difficulty of its isolation.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant lanthanide (~5.2 ppm in crust); concentrated in xenotime, monazite, bastnaesite, and ion-adsorption clays in southern China.

compounds.name: Dysprosium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale yellow-green solid with high neutron absorption; precursor for dysprosium magnets and ceramics.

compounds.name: Dysprosium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Bright yellow hygroscopic salt; precursor for dysprosium metal production by metallothermic reduction.

compounds.name: Dysprosium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used as feedstock for high-purity dysprosium metal production and in optical coatings.

compounds.name: Terfenol-D
compounds.summary: Magnetostrictive alloy of terbium, dysprosium, and iron used in transducers and actuators.

uses: Additive in NdFeB permanent magnets to maintain coercivity at elevated temperatures (EV traction motors, wind turbines)
Magnetostrictive component of Terfenol-D for transducers and active vibration control
Neutron-absorbing material in nuclear reactor control rods (dysprosium oxide-nickel cermets)
Dysprosium iodide in metal-halide arc lamps for very bright high-CRI lighting
Magnetic data-storage media (read/write heads, magneto-optical discs)
Phosphor activator in long-persistence afterglow phosphors (e.g., SrAl2O4:Eu,Dy)
reactivity_summary: Silvery-white ductile lanthanide; stable in air at room temperature but slowly oxidises and forms an oxide layer. Reacts slowly with cold water and rapidly with dilute acids; shavings can ignite in air at moderately elevated temperatures.
isotopes_summary: Dysprosium has seven naturally occurring isotopes (Dy-156, 158, 160, 161, 162, 163, 164). Dy-164 is the most abundant (~28.26%); Dy-156 and Dy-158 are very long-lived (effectively stable) alpha emitters. Dy-154 (~3x10^6 years) is the longest-lived synthetic isotope.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Dysprosium

CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd Edition
